claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 21 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can assess each one - just let me know their names and ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh hello, thanks for helping me with this! I've got three kids actually - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. 

Alex is 5, Blake is 21, and Charlie is 13. They all live with me here in Manchester. I'm hoping to claim for all of them if that's possible?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! It's lovely to help you with this. Thanks for providing all that information about Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me work through each child's eligibility for you. I'll start by checking their age categories:

- **Alex (age 5)** - Under 16 ✓
- **Blake (age 21)** - 20 or over
- **Charlie (age 13)** - Under 16 ✓

Let me navigate through the assessment for each child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! I have some important information already:

**For Blake (age 21):** I'm sorry to say that Blake is **not eligible** for Child Benefit. Children must be either under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. At 21, Blake no longer qualifies regardless of other circumstances.

---

Now let me continue the assessment for **Alex (age 5)** and **Charlie (age 13)**. Since they're both under 16, I have a couple more questions:

**1. Are Alex and Charlie both working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (5 and 13), I assume they're not working, but I just want to confirm this to proceed correctly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh gosh, no, they're not working! Alex is only 5 - she's in primary school. And Charlie is 13, still in secondary school. Neither of them have jobs or anything like that.

So Blake isn't eligible then because he's too old? That's a shame, but I understand.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes perfect sense - of course they're not working at those ages! And yes, I'm sorry about Blake, but at 21 he's unfortunately aged out of the Child Benefit system.

Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Next question:

**2. Are Alex and Charlie NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by the child themselves - not benefits that you as a parent receive for them.

At their ages (5 and 13), this would be very unusual, so I expect the answer is no, they're not receiving such benefits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - no, neither of them get any benefits in their own name. They're just kids, so it's all through me as their parent. They don't have their own claims or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**3. Are Alex and Charlie on any apprenticeships?**

At ages 5 and 13, I'd assume not - but just to confirm, neither of them are doing any kind of apprenticeship programme? (This is mainly relevant for older teenagers, but I need to check!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! Alex is only 5 and Charlie is 13 - they're both way too young for apprenticeships. Alex is in primary school and Charlie is in year 8 or 9 at secondary school. No apprenticeships at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, far too young for that! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status.

**4. Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You mentioned you live in Manchester, which is great! To confirm, do any of the following apply to you?

- **You normally live in the UK** (this sounds like your situation!)
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and are working/studying/seeking work
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

Since you said you live in Manchester, I assume you **normally live in the UK** - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I live here in Manchester. I'm a UK resident, nothing complicated about it. Just a normal resident here with my kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now let's confirm your responsibility for the children.

**5. Are you responsible for Alex and Charlie?**

This can be shown in any of these ways:
- **You live with the children** (you mentioned they all live with you in Manchester - so this sounds like it applies!)
- You contribute to their upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
- You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you said they live with you, I assume you're responsible for them by living with them - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely - they both live with me here at home. I look after them full-time, they're with me every day. I'm their primary carer.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! We're getting close now.

**6. Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- An ex-partner
- A grandparent or other family member

Is there anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either of these children, or would you be the only one?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for them. I'm the only one - it's just me and the kids. Their other parent isn't involved and isn't claiming anything for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Nearly there!

**7. Where are Alex and Charlie currently living?**

Are they:
- **Living normally at home with you** (sounds like this is the case!)
- In hospital
- In local authority care
- In a foster care arrangement

I assume they're just living at home with you as normal?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally, yes. But Charlie - he's been in care for a couple of months now. He still comes home on the weekends though, spends time with me then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that - that's really helpful information. Let me check the specific rules for Charlie's situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is really important for Charlie's situation. Let me ask a few more specific questions:

**About Charlie being in care:**

You mentioned he's been in care for a couple of months and comes home on weekends. I need to check against the specific rules:

1. **Does Charlie spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?** (For example, if he comes home Friday evening and stays until Sunday, that could potentially meet this threshold)

OR

2. **Does Charlie spend at least 2 consecutive nights per week with you, or 7 or more days in a row at home at any point?**

The reason I ask is that Child Benefit normally stops after a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks. However, there's an exception if the child regularly spends significant time at home with you - at least 24 hours per week, or 2 consecutive nights per week.

Can you tell me roughly how much time Charlie spends at home with you during his weekend visits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he definitely spends more than 24 hours a week with me. He comes home every Friday after school and stays until Sunday evening - so that's the whole weekend, Friday night, Saturday, and Sunday. That's easily more than 24 hours.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great news for Charlie's eligibility! Since he spends the whole weekend with you (Friday evening through Sunday), that's well over 24 hours per week, which means he can still qualify for Child Benefit even though he's been in care for more than 8 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check one more thing for Charlie:

**8. Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?**

This is an important question because if the council is funding Charlie's care placement (paying for his accommodation or upkeep), this can affect Child Benefit eligibility.

Do you know if the local authority is paying for Charlie's care placement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the local authority is paying for his care placement. They're covering the costs of him being in the care home during the week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being upfront about that. Unfortunately, this does affect Charlie's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to say that because the local authority is paying for Charlie's care placement, he is **not eligible** for Child Benefit at this time.

Now let me just complete the assessment for **Alex**. You mentioned she lives at home with you normally, so let me check the final question for her.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question for Alex:**

**9. Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

Since Alex lives at home with you normally, I assume the council is NOT paying for her care - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - no, the council isn't paying anything for Alex. She just lives at home with me normally, no council involvement at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! I now have all the information I need to give you a complete assessment for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 📋 Child Benefit Eligibility Summary

Here's the complete assessment for your three children:

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 5 | ✅ **YES** | Meets all eligibility criteria |
| **Blake** | 21 | ❌ No | Too old - must be under 16, or 16-19 in approved education/training |
| **Charlie** | 13 | ❌ No | Local authority is paying for his care placement |

---

### ✅ For Alex - Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using **form CH2**
2. You'll need to provide:
   - Proof of your identity
   - Alex's birth certificate
3. Payments are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

⚠️ **Note:** If you or a partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge. However, this doesn't affect your eligibility - it's just a tax adjustment.

---

### 💙 Regarding Charlie:

I'm sorry that Charlie isn't currently eligible due to the council funding his care. However, **if his situation changes** in the future - for example, if he returns home to live with you full-time and the council is no longer paying for his care - you would be able to claim Child Benefit for him at that point.

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to explain or help you with?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. She meets all the criteria: she is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for her as her primary carer, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for her, she lives at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards her accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 21) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. At 21 years old, Blake exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit. Children must be either under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. Once a child turns 20 (or 21 in Blake's case), they no longer qualify regardless of any other circumstances.

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit at this time. Although Charlie meets most criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship, you live in the UK, you are responsible for him, no one else is claiming), the local authority is paying for his care placement. When the council pays towards a child's accommodation or maintenance, Child Benefit cannot be claimed. This is the case even though Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you on weekends.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Alex is eligible for Child Benefit. Blake is too old at 21 (must be under 20 or under 16 if not in approved education/training). Charlie, although under 16 and spending significant time at home, is ineligible because the local authority is paying for his care placement during the week.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit at this time. When the council pays towards a child's accommodation or maintenance, Child Benefit cannot be claimed. This is the case even though Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you on weekends.

Charlie, although under 16 and spending significant time at home, is ineligible because the local authority is paying for his care placement during the week.
